sourceSectionFilterAuditRows.sectionTitle: Aquatic Conservation and Ecology (ACE) Degree Overview
sourceSectionFilterAuditRows.rawLine: The Aquatic Conservation and Ecology (ACE) degree is about the ecology of aquatic organisms, the rivers, lakes, and oceans in which they live, and how we conserve them for the benefit of people and the planet. This degree integrates the disciplines of ecology, evolution, and quantitative sciences and applies these principles to contemporary conservation and natural resource management issues.

sourceSectionFilterAuditRows.rawLine: We have revised and updated our degree program, and discontinued the AFS major as of Autumn quarter 2025. Please see the information for the Aquatic Conservation and Ecology (ACE) Major for the newest version of our degree. The AFS major is closed to new students. Students who declared AFS before Autumn 2025 will be able to continue in the major until they graduate.

sourceSectionFilterAuditRows.rawLine: Students studying Aquatic and Fishery Sciences (AFS) learn the dynamics of freshwater and marine environments and the sustainable management of aquatic resources. Our bachelor’s degree program emphasizes experiential learning through unique lab and field research opportunities. Undergraduate students receive individualized attention and mentorship from a small supportive community, while also benefiting from the diversity of a large research institution.

sourceSectionFilterAuditRows.sectionTitle: AFS is an open major, which means that students currently registered at UW can declare this major at any time as long as they have a 2.0 cumulative grade point average. It is also transfer-friendly, allows for over 50 free electives, and can easily be combined with minors and double degrees. Learn more about the AFS Major requirements and scholarship opportunities available.

requirementCueLines: (max. 7 credits) This is optional, but recommended for students who intend to pursue advanced degrees.

ACE is an open major, which means that students currently registered at UW can declare this major at any time as long as they have a 2.0 cumulative grade point average. It is also transfer-friendly, allows for over 50 free electives, and can easily be combined with minors and double degrees. Learn more about the ACE Major requirements and scholarship opportunities available.

AFS is an open major, which means that students currently registered at UW can declare this major at any time as long as they have a 2.0 cumulative grade point average. It is also transfer-friendly, allows for over 50 free electives, and can easily be combined with minors and double degrees. Learn more about the AFS Major requirements and scholarship opportunities available.
First Year Students: composition, calculus, chemistry, biology, FISH 200 or FISH 250.
Or students can complete independent study credits through either or a combination of the following:
Second Year Students: statistics, ACE intro coursework, Communicating Science coursework, College of Environment’s General Education requirements.
Students can complete the SAFS Capstone (review the Capstone Page for more information):

Students studying Aquatic and Fishery Sciences (AFS) learn the dynamics of freshwater and marine environments and the sustainable management of aquatic resources. Our bachelor’s degree program emphasizes experiential learning through unique lab and field research opportunities. Undergraduate students receive individualized attention and mentorship from a small supportive community, while also benefiting from the diversity of a large research institution.
Understand and appreciate the liberal arts and social sciences. Our degree requirements incorporate a range of complementary subjects offered across campus that encourages breadth as well as depth.
